Setup script line 46: observations count=80; lines 29 confirm startobs=1985:1 endobs=2004:4

pi_items.metadata_value: FRED GDPC1 (Real GDP in Billions of Chained 2012 Dollars)
pi_items.verified_value: FRED GDPC1 exists and represents Real GDP (currently in Chained 2017 Dollars)
pi_items.source: Web verified at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/GDPC1; Hardcoded data values do not match current FRED data (ratio ~0.866)

pi_items.metadata_value: FRED PCECC96 (Real PCE in Billions of Chained 2012 Dollars)
pi_items.verified_value: FRED PCECC96 exists and represents Real Personal Consumption Expenditures (currently in Chained 2017 Dollars)
pi_items.source: Web verified at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/PCECC96; Hardcoded data values do not match current FRED data (ratio ~0.919)

pi_items.verified_value: Both FRED series currently use Chained 2017 Dollars base year
pi_items.source: Current FRED documentation shows updated base year to 2017; Setup script claims 2012

pi_items.verified_value: Hardcoded values do not match real FRED data: GDPC1 off by 13.4%, PCECC96 off by 8.1%
pi_items.source: Calculated log values: hardcoded exp(8.8920)=7273.55 vs FRED GDPC1 1985Q1=8400.82; hardcoded exp(8.4951)=4890.75 vs FRED PCECC96 1985Q1=5321.159
Verified in setup_task.sh line 18 and export_result.sh line 10

pi_items.verified_value: 10 lines is a reasonable minimum for econometric analysis output
pi_items.source: Verifier.py line 65 checks for this minimum; standard econometric output would exceed this

pi_items.verified_value: ["adf", "unit root", "cointegrat"] expected in output for successful task
pi_items.source: Verifier.py lines 74-95 checks for these keywords in output file

pi_items.verified_value: Range 0.7-1.3 is theoretically plausible for consumption-income elasticity
pi_items.source: Economic theory suggests long-run elasticity should be close to 1.0; range accommodates measurement variation

pi_items.verified_value: Critical values for Engle-Granger test are approximately -2.47 (10%), -2.78 (5%), -3.37 (1%)
pi_items.source: Web search of econometric references and Gretl documentation; -2.0 is ABOVE critical values and would indicate NO cointegration, contradicting task objective

pi_items.metadata_value: Task expects evidence of cointegration between lgdp and lcons
pi_items.verified_value: If truly cointegrated, test statistic should be MORE negative than critical values (typically -3 to -5 range)
pi_items.source: Economic theory and standard econometric references; US consumption and GDP are known to be cointegrated

privileged_info_summary: The task uses two real FRED data series: GDPC1 (Real GDP) and PCECC96 (Real Personal Consumption Expenditures) for 80 quarterly observations from 1985 Q1 to 2004 Q4. The dataset variables are correctly named lgdp and lcons. However, the hardcoded data values in the task setup do not match current FRED data (off by 8-13%), suggesting either outdated data or approximation. More critically, the metadata claims an expected test statistic maximum of -2.0, which contradicts econometric theory: for cointegrated series, the Engle-Granger test statistic should be significantly more negative (typically -3 to -5) than critical values (-2.47 to -3.37), not less negative than -2.0.
